segments.s249-g81: کزین is a fused prepositional phrase 'کِ زِ + این' meaning 'from/for this'. خواسته is a noun 'request/desire' governed by that preposition. بر تو تیمار نیست is an existential‑style clause: بر (on/upon) + تو (you) + تیمار (care/worry) + نیست (is not), literally 'upon you care is not' = 'you have no concern.' Note Persian word order places the negated copula نیست at the end.
segments.s249-g82: This is a parallel clause giving the same idea in a different phrasing. کسی را introduces the (non‑existent) person — را here is emphatic/topic marking attached to کسی. بدین here functions as 'به + این' (with/respect to this), but in the provided word split the element دین does not correspond to that meaning (see senses). با تو پیکار نیست is 'there is no one to fight with you': با تو (with you) + پیکار (fight/battle) + نیست (does not exist). The negation is expressed by نیست at the end.
segments.s249-g83: Imperative sequence: برو (go) is the simple imperative. هرچ داری = 'whatever (هر چه) you have' — هرچ (whatever) + داری (you have, present). بهایی بیار is another imperative: بهایی (valuables/priceable things) + بیار (bring), i.e. 'bring your valuables.' Note Persian frequently strings imperatives and clauses without a coordinating particle.
segments.s249-g84: خریدار کن is an idiomatic imperative: خریدار (buyer/taker) + کن (make/do) — 'get/find a buyer'. هر سوی خواستار is a locative/qualifying phrase: هر (every/any) + سوی (side/direction) = 'on every side/wherever', with خواستار (desirous/bidder) indicating there are suitors; the phrase modifies where to seek a buyer.
segments.s249-g85: فرود آی is an imperative of descent: فرود (descent/coming down) + آی (come!) used together as 'come down!' در خان = 'into the khan/house/inn' (در = in/into; خان here = building/house/inn). فرزند من = 'my son' (possessive expressed by juxtaposition + من). The phrase is an imperative invitation to enter.
segments.s249-g86: چنان باش با من is an imperative formula: چنان ('so/thus/such') + باش (be!) + با من (with me). The clause introduced by که (that/so that) begins a relative/purpose clause describing how to be: 'be such with me that ...' پیوند من 'my bond' follows and is the semantic target of that clause (the continuation likely completes the thought).
segments.s249-g87: بدو گفت رستم is word order variant for 'رستم گفت بدو' — بدو (to him) is the indirect object 'to him', گفت is 'said' (here the past-tense verb 'گفت' heads the speech report), and رستم is the speaker's name. که‌ای پهلوان is an archaic vocative: که‌ای (que‑ī) functions like 'O' + vocative noun پهلوان (hero/champion).
segments.s249-g88: هم ایدر بباشیم با کاروان is the reported command. هم here = 'also/indeed' or emphatic 'do also', ایدر = 'here' (archaic spelling), بباشیم is an optative/imperative form meaning 'remain/stay' (1st‑person‑plural form used as hortative/imperative in older Persian contexts), با کاروان = 'with the caravan'. Together: 'Remain here with the caravan.'
segments.s249-g17: بلشکر ('the commander' or 'the army‑leader') is the speaker noun placed before the verb. چنین گفت is a fixed phrase meaning 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'said' — past tense), and پس پهلوان is a vocative sequence: پس here works as 'then' or a linking particle and پهلوان is the addressee 'champions' (vocative). The whole segment introduces the reported speech.
segments.s249-g18: که introduces the clause that follows (here functioning like 'that' / 'who' or linking the direct speech to the preceding words). ایدر is an archaic/place word meaning 'here'. بباشید is the 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive of بودن used as 'be/stay' (بَباشید = 'remain' or 'be here'). روشن‌روان is an epithet (adjective/noun) 'bright‑souled' or 'clear‑minded' addressing the champions. Together: 'that you stay here, bright‑souled ones.'
segments.s249-g19: مجنبید is a negative imperative (2nd person plural) formed from جنبیدن: 'do not move / do not stir.' از + ایدر = 'from here' (از = 'from', ایدر = 'here'). مگر introduces the exception/clause 'unless' that follows. جان من is the possessed noun 'my life' (جان = 'life/soul', من = possessive 'my' here). The segment issues the order 'Do not move from here, unless my life...'.
segments.s249-g20: ز تن = 'from the body' (ز is the poetic variant of از). بگسلد is a verbal form here meaning 'may ... separate / be torn away' (third‑person, optative/possible subjunctive sense in this oath). پاک یزدان من is an appended oath/invocative: پاک = 'pure', یزدان = 'God/divine', من = 'my' (my pure God). Grammatically the clause is an apodosis: 'unless my life parts from my body — (by) my pure God.'
segments.s249-g21: بسیچیده باشید is an imperative/perfective instruction: بسیچیده (past participle) 'well‑arrayed/ marshalled' + باشید (2nd person plural imperative of بودن) gives 'be/stand well‑arrayed' or 'have (it) well arranged.' مر جنگ را = 'for my battle' or 'the battle for me' (مر = 'my/to me', جنگ = 'battle', را = object marker). The segment is an order to prepare the battle for the speaker.
segments.s249-g22: همه is the subject 'everyone/all'; تیز کرده is a compound of adjective + کردِه (past participle) meaning 'having made (something) sharp' — it implies 'sharpen (your weapons)'. بخون is an imperative 'sing!/play!/strike!' (here used with an instrument). چنگ را is the definite object 'the harp' (چنگ = harp, را = object marker). Together the clause pairs a martial instruction (sharpen) with the command to sound the harp (signal/encourage).
segments.s249-g23: سپه is the subject 'the army/host.' بر سر مرز is a prepositional phrase 'on the border/edge of' (بر = 'on', سر = part of the idiom 'بر سر مرز' = 'on the border of'), ایران names the country. بماند is a 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative or jussive form of ماندن 'to remain,' used as an instruction/optative: 'let it remain' or 'may it remain.' The whole segment issues the order that the army remain at Iran's frontier.
segments.s249-g24: خود 'he himself' (emphatic/reflexive), و = 'and.' سرکشان is a plural noun (from سرکش) meaning 'the rebellious/defiant ones' or 'mounted/warriors' in context — here it denotes those who will move with him. سوی توران = 'toward Turan' (سوی = toward, توران = place name). براند is from راندن 'to drive/lead/turn' (3rd‑person singular subjunctive or future‑intent sense) meaning 'will drive/turn (toward).' The clause contrasts the army that stays with the leader and his followers who move toward Turan.
segments.s249-g9: This clause names the hero (تهمتن) and a simple past verb (بیامد, "came"). چو introduces a simile here (= "like/as"); the phrase سرو بلند follows the simile and functions as its complement: "like a tall cypress." Word order is Subject — Verb — Simile Phrase (typical of epic Persian).
segments.s249-g10: بچنگ is the contracted prepositional phrase به‌چنگ "in (his) grasp/hand" and precedes the nouns it governs; اندرون is an adverbial specifying location/intonation ("within/in hand"). گرز is the object ("mace"); و links the second prepositional phrase بر زین کمند (بر = "on/upon", زین = "saddle", کمند = "lasso/snare"). Together the segment means "with a mace in his hand and a lasso on the saddle."
segments.s249-g11: سپاه is the noun subject here ("the army/host"). The phrase از پس پشت (literally "from after/behind the back") and گردان ز پیش ("the rotating/tactical squadrons from the front") describes the disposition of forces — "the army at the rear and the squadrons before (him)." Note the poetic use of prepositional/order phrases (ز + locative) to indicate positions.
segments.s249-g12: نهاده is a past participle/finite-like perfect meaning "placed/has placed" (here functioning as the main verbal idea). بکف (به‌کف) "into the hand" is an idiom meaning "into the keeping/charge of"; بر introduces the object upon which something is laid, and همه جان خویش "all his life" is the thing entrusted — note that in classical Persian خویش is often written as خوی + ش (possessive). The full sense: "(he) placed ... into their hands, having staked his entire life."
segments.s249-g13: برفت (classical past of رفتن, here meaning "he departed/left") is the past tense verb; از در شاه "from the king's gate/door" gives the source, with در used as "gate/door" and شاه as the possessor. با لشکرش "with his army" is an adjunct phrase. Word order puts the verb before the source phrase for poetic effect, and the subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s249-g14: بسی here is an adverb meaning "much/very; many" modifying آفرین ("acclaim/praise"). خواند is the past tense "he uttered/said/sang," so آفرین خواند = "he uttered acclamations/praised." برکشورش is the object ("over/for his realm/country"). The whole clause means "he gave many praises to his realm."
segments.s249-g15: چو here is temporal = "when"; نزدیکی مرز توران is a noun phrase "the vicinity/nearby of Turan's border." رسید is the past tense verb ("arrived/reached"). The sequence is a standard temporal clause: "When he reached the near border of Turan...".
segments.s249-g16: سران ("the leaders/chiefs") is the direct object, marked by the object marker را. ز لشکر همه (= "from the whole army") indicates the source/domain of selection, and برگزید is the past tense verb "he chose/selected." Word order here is Object — را — Source phrase — Verb, common in narrative Persian.
segments.s249-g41: This is a noun phrase meaning 'all the champions of Turan'. همه is a universal determiner placed before the noun group; پهلوانان is the plural noun 'champions'; توران follows as a postpositive modifier ('of Turan'). بجای here is a locative/adverbial forming a phrase 'in (their) places' that will link to the following verbal idea (they took their stations).
segments.s249-g42: شده is a past participial/finite form meaning 'became/was done' or here simply part of the past narrative ('(they) were/assembled'); پیش is the preposition 'before/in the presence of'; پیران ویسه is the personal name 'Piran Viseh' (here functioning as the person before whom they stand); بپای is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'to stand/abide/keep watch' (so the clause says they 'stood' before him). The sequence shows the locative phrase from the previous segment realized: they took their places and stood before Piran Viseh.
segments.s249-g43: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; پیران ویسه is the name 'Piran Viseh' as the subject of the coming action; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; نخچیر گاه is a compound meaning 'hunting-ground' (نخچیر 'game/prey' + گاه 'place/time'), so the whole segment means 'when Piran Viseh came from the hunting-ground.'
segments.s249-g44: بیامد is the simple past 'he came' (here referring back to Piran Viseh); the next clause gives the reaction: تهمتن is the name 'Tahmatan' (Rostam), بدیدش is the past verb 'saw him' with the enclitic pronoun -ش 'him' (referring to Piran), and براه is a locative/adverb 'on the road'/'along the way'. Word order separates the two clauses: 'he came; Tahmatan saw him on the road.'
segments.s249-g45: This noun phrase describes the object: یکی is the indefinite numeral/article 'one/a certain'; جام 'cup' followed by the adjective زرین 'golden' (classical Persian allows adjective after noun); پر از گوهرا is a postnominal phrase 'full of jewels' (پر 'full (of)' + از 'of' + گوهرا 'jewels'), qualifying the cup.
segments.s249-g46: بدیبا is an adverb 'marvellously' modifying the verb بیاراست/بپوشید-like action; بپوشید here is a past verbal form 'covered/dressed/arrayed' (used transitively: they adorned/covered something); رستم سرا is a genitival phrase meaning 'Rostam's house/household' (رستم 'Rostam' + سرا 'house'), the object/location that is being splendidly adorned. In classical narrative the agent can be omitted, so the verb appears without an explicit subject.
segments.s249-g47: This noun group names the animals: (ده) is the numeral 'ten' (present in the line but not listed in the word items), اسب 'horse' + گرانمایه 'precious/esteemed' describes their value; با introduces the accompaniment 'with' and زیورش is زیور 'adornment/jewelry' with the enclitic -ش 'their' (their trappings). Together: 'ten fine horses with their trappings.'
segments.s249-g48: بدیبا is the adverb 'marvellously' again; بیاراست is the past verb 'adorned/arrayed' (transitive or causative 'made beautiful'); اندر is the archaic locative preposition 'in/within'; خورش is the noun naming the place where they were set (here 'stable/pen' in context). The clause therefore says he 'marvellously arrayed them within the stable.'
segments.s249-g33: صد اشتر (literally “a hundred [ashṭar]”) is a numeral phrase; here اشتر is the counted noun ‘camels’ (classical Persian allows numeral + noun without plural). همه بار او گوهرا is a noun phrase where همه 'whole/entire' modifies بار 'load' and او is the genitive pronoun 'his' placed after the noun (classical order: noun + possessive). گوهرا is the plural/collective form of گوهر, ‘gems/jewels’, specifying the content of his load.
segments.s249-g34: The second half repeats the numeral phrase صد اشتر 'a hundred camels' followed by همه جامه 'all the garments' (همه again = 'whole/entire' modifying جامه). لشکرا is a classical genitive/attributive form indicating the garments belong to or are for the army (لشکر). The clause is a paratactic listing of loads carried by camels.
segments.s249-g35: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/with' introducing accompanying noises. بس‌های is a plural/intensive use of بس ('enough/abundant') indicating many or much; و هوی و درنگ strings together nouns 'and hūy (shouts/ cries) and pauses/delays.' درای is the adjectival/participial form meaning 'having/possessing' (classical variant of دارا), linking this descriptive material to what follows.
segments.s249-g36: بکردار is an epithet meaning 'virtuous' or 'of good character' and here modifies the following proper name. تهمورثی is the name (Tahmuras) in a dependent form; کرنای is the noun 'trumpets/horns' (in classical Persian the modifier and possessor often follow the head noun), so the phrase reads 'the trumpets of Tahmuras the virtuous.'
segments.s249-g37: همی is the frequentative/progressive particle used with past verbs ('kept on / was'). شهر بر شهر is an idiomatic repetitive phrase 'city upon city / from city to city' with بر here meaning toward/to. هودج is the direct object 'palanquin/sedan chair', and کشید is the past tense of کشیدن 'to pull/draw' (classical pale form used transitively), so the clause means 'he pulled/drew the palanquin from city to city.'
segments.s249-g38: Again همی marks continued action; رفت is the simple past 'went.' تا introduces the limit 'until' and governs the following clause شهر توران رسید 'he reached the city of Turan' where رسید is the past of رسیدن 'arrived/reached.' The sequence expresses motion up to the arrival point.
segments.s249-g39: چو آمد is a classical temporal clause 'when he came.' بنزدیک is an adverb/compound meaning 'near/close to' and precedes شهر ختن, where شهر names the place and ختن here denotes the proper name Khotan (a city), not the literal verb sense given in the lemma list.
segments.s249-g40: نظارَه بیامد is an impersonal classical phrasing meaning 'a sight/scene came (into view)' or 'people came to look.' بیامد is a past form 'came.' برش is an archaic/cliticized locative/benefactive 'before him / at him' (بر + pronoun). مرد و زن follows as the agents/participants 'men and women' — i.e., men and women came to look.
segments.s249-g65: اگر begins a conditional clause ('if'). پهلوان is the subject 'champion/hero'. گیردم is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'takes me' (گیرد + enclitic object -م = 'me'); the verb appears inside the protasis (the 'if' clause). زیر بر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'under / subject to (his) power' (زیر = 'under', بر kept with it in poetic word order). Word order here is typical of epic poetry: condition marker + subject + verb + postpositional phrase.
segments.s249-g66: This is a nominative/accusative list of things offered or given. خرم is an adjective 'cheerful/pleasant/fine' placed before the noun چارپای 'quadruped' (here a horse/steed). و links the second item; فروشم is a poetic/fossilized form related to 'فروش' (here best read as 'what I will sell' or 'my sale/for sale'), followed by گهر 'a gem'. Together: 'my fine steed and (I) will sell my jewel.'
segments.s249-g67: هم here begins the first part of a correlative construction (هم ... هم ... = 'neither ... nor / both ... and'); از introduces the object 'from' or 'about'. داد is 'justice' and تو is the possessor 'your'. کس is 'anyone/someone' and نیازاردم is an archaic first-person verbal/nominal form meaning 'I would beg/ask' or 'I make supplication'. The clause reads literally 'I would not/ I will not ask anyone for your justice.'
segments.s249-g68: The second correlative clause: هم 'and/also', از + ابر مهرت = 'from the cloud of your favour' (ابر 'cloud' + مهرت 'your favour/kindness' with poetic word order). گهر 'gems' is the thing concerned, and باردم is a first‑person verbal form of باریدن ('I rained / I made fall') used metaphorically: 'I caused jewels to fall.' In poetry the verb form is elliptical and the subject (I) is understood.
segments.s249-g69: پس 'then' opens the narrative continuation. آن جام 'that cup' is the head noun; پر + گوهر is a adjectival phrase 'full of gems' (پر = 'full (of)', complemented by گوهر), and شاهوار is a further attributive adjective 'kingly/majestic'. Adjectives follow in a stack after the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s249-g70: میان کیان = 'among the kings' (میان = 'among / in the midst of', کیان = 'kings'). کرد is the simple past/formal verb 'he made/did/placed'. پیشش 'before him / in his presence' (prepositional/locative), and نثار is a noun 'offering' so the verb + noun sequence means 'he made it an offering before him' (i.e. offered it). The verb carries the action while the object is expressed as 'نثار' (offering).
segments.s249-g71: An adjectival noun phrase: گرانمایه 'precious/high-priced' modifies اسبان 'horses'. تازی‌نژاد is a compound adjective 'of Tazi (Arab) descent' (i.e. Arab‑bred). The whole phrase names costly Arab horses.
segments.s249-g72: Relative clause introduced by که 'which/that' modifying 'horses'. بر مویشان = 'upon their manes' (بر = 'on/upon', مویشان = 'their hair/manes'). گرد = 'dust' and نفشاند is a verb 'scattered/strewn' (past or perfective sense). باد appears at the end as the grammatical subject displaced for poetic word order ('the wind scattered dust on their manes') — in classical Persian wind can also carry an optative nuance, but here it reads naturally as the agent 'wind'.
segments.s249-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when,” introducing a time clause. سالار نوبت is a compound noun phrase (سالار 'commander' + نوبت 'of the turn/shift') that serves as the subject; بیامد is the past-tense 3rd-person singular verb 'came/arrived.' بدر is the proper name placed after the verb as an appositive/subject (poetic word order places the name after the verb).
segments.s249-g2: بشبگیر is an adverbial/temporal phrase 'for the night' / 'at nightfall.' بستند is the past 3rd-plural 'they fastened/tied' (here an action taken by the troops). گردان کمر is an object phrase where گردان ('troop') qualifies کمر ('girdle/belt') — i.e. 'the troop's belts' — so the clause means they girded/tied the troop's belts (prepared themselves) for the night.
segments.s249-g3: همه is the quantifier 'all.' نیزه داران is a compound meaning 'holders of spears' (spearmen); جنگ آوران is a parallel noun phrase 'war-bringers' = 'fighters/warriors.' The two nominal phrases are put in parataxis (a poetic list) without a conjunction between the noun and its epithet.
segments.s249-g4: Again همه 'all' quantifies the following group. مرزبانان is plural 'border-guards/frontier defenders' and ناماوران is an epithet 'renowned/illustrious ones' placed after as apposition; the construction lists another class of fighters described as famed.
segments.s249-g5: همه 'all' quantifies the whole list. نیزه و تیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'spears and arrows.' بار هیون is a genitive/possessive-like phrase 'the load of the beasts' (بار 'load' qualified by هیون 'beasts'), so the phrase reads 'all the spears and arrows that were a burden/carried by the beasts.'
segments.s249-g6: This clause is verbless (a common poetic ellipsis): همه quantifies people/things previously named; جنگ را uses the object marker را with جنگ 'the battle' to make the scene definite; دست شسته is a participial adjective 'hands washed' used predicatively, and بخون is a prepositional-type phrase (ب + خون) 'with/in blood.' Together the clause means '[they] in the battle had their hands washed in blood' (i.e. their hands were soaked/stained with blood).
segments.s249-g7: سپیده دمان is a time expression 'dawn's edge / break of day' (سپیده 'dawn' + دمان 'edge/hem' used figuratively); گاه is the noun 'time/occasion' reinforcing the temporal setting. بانگ خروس is a noun phrase 'the cry/sound of the cock' (the cockcrow as a morning signal).
segments.s249-g8: ببستند is past 3rd-plural of بستن; in archaic/poetic usage 'بستن کوس' can mean to sound/prepare the war-drum, so here بستند refers to the action performed on the drum. بر introduces the place 'upon,' کوهه 'on the mountain,' and پیل کوس is a compound epithet 'elephant (پیل) drum (کوس)' = 'the great/huge drum.' The clause therefore describes them striking/sounding the great drum on the mountain.
segments.s249-g49: بفرمانبران داد و خود پیش رفت is a compact clause: بفرمانبران is a beneficiary/indirect object formed with the prefix بـ (classical contraction of به) + فرمانبران (“those who obey/obedient ones”), and داد is the past tense transitive verb “gave.” و connects clauses. خود is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun “himself,” and پیش رفت is the verbal phrase “went forward/proceeded” (پیش as an adverb ‘forward/in front,’ رفت past ‘went’).
segments.s249-g50: بدرگاه introduces a locative phrase “in/into the presence (of the court)”; پیران is the plural subject/complement “the elders” (or spiritual elders). خرامید is a past tense verb meaning “strode/proudly advanced.” تفت here is a short poetic particle/exclamation that colours the verb (“with a proud flourish”) rather than its literal senses in ordinary lexicons.
segments.s249-g51: برو آفرین کرد is an idiomatic reporting phrase: آفرین کردن = “to praise/exclaim ‘bravo’.” برو here functions as an exclamatory/introductory particle attached to the utterance, and کرد is the past-tense verb “did/uttered.” کای is the vocative “O, Kay,” and نامور is an epithet placed after the vocative meaning “renowned/famed.”
segments.s249-g52: This short clause expresses a wish or benediction with an omitted verb (common in classical Persian). بایران and توران name the two lands (here read as “Iran and Turan”); ببخت and هنر are the two items wished — “(may there be) luck/fortune and art/virtue.” The conjunction و links the elements; the verb such as باشد/آوردن is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s249-g53: چنان کرد is a fixed phrase “he did so/so he made (it).” روی + ش = رویش is “his face/visage” (رویش = روی + the possessive suffix ش). جهاندار ساز is a compound: جهاندار “sovereign/conqueror” followed by ساز (here functioning as a causative/performative element ‘to make/shape as’ in poetic diction), meaning “to make (him) appear a world‑ruler.” Word order is inverted for poetic effect: the result (جهاندار ساز) qualifies the action on رویش.
segments.s249-g54: که introduces the subordinate clause “so that/that.” پیران is the subject “the elders.” مر is an archaic/clitic form meaning “me” (similar to مرا) used here in classical narration; او را is the object “him” (را marks definite object). ندانست is the past negative of دانستن ‘know’ → “did not know/recognize,” and باز is an adverb “again.” Together: “that the elders did not know him again (for me / as I saw).”
segments.s249-g55: بپرسید is the past tense third person plural ‘they asked.’ و is the coordinating conjunction. گفت here is the past-reporting verb “they said.” از کجایی is the direct interrogative phrase “from where are you?” and بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form meaning “tell (us)” or “speak,” used to introduce the requested answer.
segments.s249-g56: چه مردی is an interrogative “what kind of man are you?” (چه = what/which; مردی = man / manliness). و = “and.” چون آمدی is “how/ in what manner did you come?” (چون used as ‘how’ + آمدی past ‘you came’). پوی پوی is a reduplicated adverbial “swift(ly), swift(ly)” stressing speed — repetition for emphasis is common in poetry.
segments.s249-g73: بسی (an adverb) intensifies the following verb ('very/greatly'). آفرین کرد is a two-word verbal phrase where آفرین is a noun/nominal exclamation used as the object of کردن (here 'to offer praise' or 'to acclaim') and کرد is the past tense 3rd-person singular of کردن ('he did/offered'); the coordinating و links this clause to what follows. آن خواسته is a demonstrative + noun ('that request/wish') and functions as the object of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s249-g74: بدو داد is a past-tense clause: داد (past of دادن) 'gave' with the enclitic/compound pronoun بدو 'to him/unto it' marking the indirect object. The conjunction و continues the sentence. شد here is the past of شدن used as a copular/inchoative verb 'became' (not modal), followed by کار آراسته where کار is the subject/nominal ('affair/matter') and آراسته is a postpositive adjectival participle 'adorned/arranged' describing کار.
segments.s249-g75: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. پیران is the plural noun 'the elders'. The sequence ب + دان is an older/poetic realization of به/آن (see note) functioning as a locative/demonstrative 'at/that' (here attaching to گوهران: 'those jewels'). بنگرید is a classical third-person past/prehistoric perfect form of نگرستن ('they looked/gazed/considered') and takes پیران (the agents) and بدان گوهران (the thing looked at).
segments.s249-g76: کزان is a compound relative 'from which' (ک + ز + آن). جام is the noun 'cup' and رخشنده is an adjective/participle 'radiant/bright' modifying جام. آمد is the past tense 'came' and پدید is an adverbial/verb meaning 'to appear' — the poetic order آمد پدید corresponds to 'came into view / appeared', a common inverted verbal phrase in classical Persian.
segments.s249-g77: برو here is a brief narrative particle/verb-form used in poetry (literally 'go' or 'thereupon') introducing the next action. آفرین کرد repeats the pattern 'he praised' (آفرین as acclamation, کرد past 'he did'). وبنواختش is a compound: the conjunction و plus بنواختش (بنواخت + ـش). بنواخت (from نواختن) in literary usage can mean 'struck/played' (as on an instrument) or 'struck/treated', and the enclitic ـش is the object pronoun 'it/him'.
segments.s249-g78: بران is the fused prepositional phrase بر + آن 'upon that'. تخت is the noun 'throne' and پیروزه is an adjective (here the turquoise-colored or turquoise-inlaid epithet) modifying تخت. بنشاختش is a past tense verb (بنشاخت + ـش) from نشاختن/نشاندن meaning 'placed/ set up'; the suffix ـش again is the object pronoun 'it' (i.e. placed it on the throne).
segments.s249-g79: که here introduces a subordinate / purposive clause 'so that' (common in classical poetic syntax). رو شاد is a nominal phrase 'face happy' (رو = 'face/appearance', شاد = 'glad'), and و ایمن adds 'and safe/secure'. بشهر اندرا is a locative phrase 'in the city of Andra' (بشهر = 'in/at the city', اندرا a proper name). Together the clause expresses purpose/result: 'so that his face be joyful and secure in Andra's city.'
segments.s249-g80: کنون is an adverb 'now/currently'. نزد here is the preposition 'near/close to' (or 'in the presence of'). خویشت in classical Persian often denotes 'self/one's own person' (here treated as the proximate referent 'himself' or 'his person' in the sense 'near him'); بسازیم is present-subjunctive/optative 1st-person plural of ساختن 'let us/ we will make/prepare', and جا is the object 'a place'. The whole clause therefore: 'now let us prepare a place near him.'
segments.s249-g57: بدو is the prepositional contraction به او (“to him/unto him”) and introduces the indirect object; گفت is the past tense verb “said.” The subject رستم follows the verb (poetic inversion), and ترا is the accusative/oblique form of تو (“you”); کهترم is an adjective (کهتر “inferior/lesser”) with the first‑person suffix -م, giving the predicate “I am lesser.”
segments.s249-g58: بشهر = به شهر (“to/into the city”) + تو (“your”/“your city”) is the place phrase; ایزد is the agent/nominative “God,” and کرد is the simple past of کردن “made/did.” آبشخورم is آبشخور “watering‑place/watering‑trough” with the first‑person possessive suffix (my), so the clause literally reads “God made (your) city my watering‑place.” Note that in epic diction such a past tense may express a wish/blessing (‘may God make’).
segments.s249-g59: ببازارگانی is an archaic/poetic nominal/adverbial form meaning ‘as a trader/for trade’; ز ایران is the prepositional phrase “from Iran” (ز = from), and بتور is the adverb بتور/به طور (“in this manner/thus”). The sequence places the speaker’s social function and origin before the main verb.
segments.s249-g60: بپیمودم is the past tense first‑person singular ‘I traversed/covered’ (ب‑ prefix + پیمودم); این راه دشوار و دور is the direct object phrase ‘this road, difficult and far,’ where دشوار and دور are coordinate adjectives linked by و. Word order places the verb before the object in typical epic/poetic inversion.
segments.s249-g61: فروشنده‌ام combines فروشنده “seller/trader” with the first‑person copular suffix -ام ‘I am.’ هم ... نیز frames a parallel pair: هم خریدار نیز = ‘and also a buyer,’ using both هم and نیز for emphatic coordination.
segments.s249-g62: فروشم here functions as the selling/sale side (a noun/summary of activity) while بخرم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form ‘I buy’ (in classical/poetic usage parallel actions can be tersely juxtaposed). ز هر گونه چیز is a prepositional phrase ‘of/from every kind of thing,’ where ز = ‘of/from,’ هر گونه = ‘every kind,’ and چیز = ‘thing.’ Overall the clause states the speaker both sells and buys all sorts of things.
segments.s249-g63: بمهر تو = به مهر تو (‘by/with your kindness/affection’), an instrumental/causal prepositional phrase. دارم is the present/stative verb ‘I have/hold,’ and روان را نوید is the object phrase ‘a promise for (my) soul’ with را marking the definite/accusative object (روان = soul, نوید = promise/omen). The whole segment says the speaker carries/possesses in his soul a pledge prompted by the addressee’s kindness.
segments.s249-g64: چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so.’ چیره شد is the compound ‘became dominant/overcame’ (چیره as adjective/participle + شد ‘became’). بر دلم is the locative ‘upon my heart’ (بر = upon; دل + م = دلم ‘my heart’ — note in the segmentation the -م appears as a separate item but functionally it is the possessive suffix ‘my’ attached to دل). بر امید (‘upon hope’) closes the phrase: hope took hold on his heart. Together: ‘thus hope became dominant on my heart.’
segments.s249-g97: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from every side' (ز + هر + سو). خریدار is the (collective) subject 'buyer(s)'. بنهاد is an archaic/poetic past verb form ('put/placed' — here used idiomatically) and گوش is the object; together بنهاد گوش = 'laid/set (their) ear' i.e. 'lent an ear / pricked up their ears'. Word order is prepositional phrase → subject → verb → object, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s249-g98: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی آمد is a light impersonal clause 'information/news came/arrived'. ز introduces the source 'from', and گوهر فروش is a compound 'jewel-seller' (گوهر = jewel, فروش = seller). The whole clause means 'when news came from the jeweler.'
segments.s249-g99: This noun phrase lists the people and the goods: خریدار is the (collective) subject 'the buyers', followed by the coordinated direct objects دیبا و فرش و گهر ('brocade and carpets and jewels'). The goods are listed before the verb which follows in the next segment.
segments.s249-g100: بدرگاه پیران is an adverbial/locative phrase 'in the presence/at the court of the elders' (بدرگاه = in the presence / at court; پیران = elders). نهادند is past tense, third-person plural 'they placed/set' (note the plural -ند). سر is the object 'head' and in the idiom نهادن سر 'to lay the head' = 'to bow' or 'to humble oneself.'
segments.s249-g101: چو introduces a temporal/similar clause 'when/as'. خورشید is 'the sun' used as a simile ('like the sun'). گیتی is the object 'the world'. بیاراستی is an archaic/poetic past form (second-person singular) meaning 'you adorned/arrayed' (be- prefix + آراستن), so the clause reads 'when you arrayed the world like the sun.' Note the second-person ending -ی marking 'you' in classical Persian.
segments.s249-g102: بدان is the fused locative 'to/into that' (ب = to; دان here represents the demonstrative 'آن' in the manuscript/segmentation), introducing the destination کلبه بازار 'market hut/stall'. برخاستی is past tense, second-person singular 'you rose/rose up/rose and departed' — here meaning 'you rose/moved toward' that stall. The verb's -ی ending marks the archaic second-person singular.
segments.s249-g89: که here introduces a causal/subordinate clause ("since/because"). Word order is poetic: با ما ز هر گونه مردم بود = literally "with us from every kind people was," i.e. "there were people of every sort with us." بود is a past-tense (existential/copular) verb meaning "there was."
segments.s249-g90: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning "it must not / ought not (to be)" and governs a following that-clause introduced by که. زان is an anaphoric/pointing pronoun meaning "from that / that (thing)" and here refers back to the jewel; گوهری is "jewel," گم "lost/absent". بود is the copula used in the subordinate clause (archaic/classical use: the past form is used for the state 'to be' in such clauses).
segments.s249-g91: بدو گفت = "he said to him" (بدو = to him). رو here functions imperatively/verbally as "turn/face" (face toward); بآرزو is the prepositional phrase 'به آرزو' contracted to "toward desire/where you wish" (آرزو = wish/desire). گیر is imperative from گرفتن meaning "take," and جای = "place/position." The phrase orders two imperatives: face toward X and take a place.
segments.s249-g92: کنم is first-person singular present/future sense "I will do / I will make" (here: 'I will act'). رهنمایی is a noun meaning "guidance" or "a guide." بپیشت and بپای are contracted, poetic prepositional phrases: بپیشت = به پیشِ تو ("before you/ahead of you") and بپای = به پایِ تو ("at your feet" or "by you/at your side"). Together they mean "I will guide you ahead of you and be at your feet," i.e. I will lead/accompany you.
segments.s249-g93: یکی here = "someone/a certain person." خانه بگزید = "chose a house" (بگزید past of گزیدن = chose). و بر ساخت کار = "and upon it he made/built work" — بر + ساخت expresses building/raising something on or over it; ساخت is a past participle/verb "built/made." The clause follows classical poetic order where objects and modifiers often precede the verb.
segments.s249-g94: بکلبه = به کلبه (to the cottage/hut). درون رخت بنهاد = literally "inside (درون) he placed (بنهاد) his rakh(t)," where رخت in this context means "baggage/gear" rather than simply 'clothing.' و بار = "and the load/burden" (بار = load) — overall: he put his baggage inside the cottage and set down the load. بنهاد is past tense 'placed.'
segments.s249-g95: خبر شد is an impersonal construction meaning "news came / it became known." کز introduces the content clause "that/...from which" (here equivalent to 'that'). کز ایران یکی کاروان = "a caravan from Iran" (یکی = a certain). The clause reports the content of the news.
segments.s249-g96: بیامد is past tense "came/arrived." بر here is a classical/prepositional use meaning "to/toward" (i.e., arriving at someone), and نامور پهلوان = "the renowned/famed champion." Together: "(they) came to the famed champion."
segments.s249-g25: This is an imperative address to the merchants: the object phrase 'همه جامه' ('all the garments') precedes the imperative verb 'برسان' ('bring/deliver'), and 'بازارگان' at the end is vocative ('merchants'). The verb form 'برسان' functions as a command here (classical/poetic imperative).
segments.s249-g26: 'بپوشید' is a 2nd-person plural imperative ('put on' / 'clothe [them]'). The conjunction 'و' links two commands, and 'بگشاد' is used as an instruction 'open/loosen' (here an imperative or hortative command). 'بند از میان' is a prepositional phrase 'the strap/band from the middle' with 'از میان' meaning 'from the middle.'
segments.s249-g27: گشادند is a predicative plural ('they were wide/broad'); the following noun group 'گردان کمرهای سیم' supplies the subject (literally 'the troop's silver kempers/belts'). Word order places the predicate before the subject for a poetic effect.
segments.s249-g28: 'بپوشیدشان' is an imperative verb 'clothe/put on' with the enclitic object pronoun '-شان' ('them') attached, so 'clothe them.' The direct object 'جامه‌های گلیم' ('kilim garments') follows and specifies what they are to be clothed in.
segments.s249-g29: The prepositional phrase 'سوی شهر توران' ('toward the city of Turan') precedes the verb; 'نهادند' is the past-tense verb (3rd-person plural) from 'نهادن' meaning 'set/placed' and here part of the idiom 'روی نهادن' = 'to turn one's face toward.' 'روی' is the object of that idiom meaning 'face.'
segments.s249-g30: This is a nominal apposition describing what moved: 'یکی کاروانی' ('one/a certain caravan') followed by the adjectival phrase 'پر از رنگ و بوی' ('full of color and scent'). 'پر از' is the standard construction 'full of X.'
segments.s249-g31: This noun phrase 'گرانمایه هفت اسب با کاروان' places the adjective 'گرانمایه' ('precious') before the numeral and noun 'هفت اسب' ('seven horses'), and the prepositional phrase 'با کاروان' ('with the caravan') indicates accompaniment. The whole functions as the subject of the following statement.
segments.s249-g32: 'یکی رخش' is an elliptical clause common in Persian poetry where the copula is omitted: 'one (was) Rakhsh.' The conjunction 'و' connects to 'دیگر' ('the other'), and 'نشست' is a past tense verb 'sat/was seated'; 'گوان' is an adverb 'thus/like(wise),' so the second clause reads 'and the other sat likewise.'

sentences.s249-s41: Because of this request you have nothing to worry about; no one will engage in battle with you over it.
sentences.s249-s42: Go; bring whatever valuables you have; find a buyer wherever there is a bidder.
sentences.s249-s43: Come down into the house, my son; be with me in such a way as befits my bond.
sentences.s249-s44: Rostam said to him, 'O champion, remain here with the caravan.'
sentences.s249-s9: The commander said thus: "Then, champions, remain here, O bright‑souled ones."
sentences.s249-s10: Do not stir from here, unless — may my life be torn from my body, by my pure God.
sentences.s249-s11: Array the battle well for me; everyone, having made (your weapons) sharp, strike (pluck) the harp's strings.
sentences.s249-s12: Let the army remain on the frontier of Iran; he himself and the (سرکشان) shall turn toward Turan.
sentences.s249-s5: Tahmatan came like a tall cypress, with a mace in his hand and a lasso on the saddle.
sentences.s249-s6: He marshalled the army behind and the squadrons in front, laying his whole life in their hands.
sentences.s249-s7: He left the king’s gate with his host, and loudly praised his realm.
sentences.s249-s8: When he reached the vicinity of Turan’s border, he selected the chiefs from the whole army.
sentences.s249-s21: All the champions of Turan took their places and stood before Piran Viseh.
sentences.s249-s22: When Piran Viseh came from the hunting-ground, Tahmatan saw him on the road.
sentences.s249-s23: A single golden cup, full of jewels — and Rostam's house was marvelously adorned.
sentences.s249-s24: Ten prized horses, with their trappings, he marvellously arrayed in the stable.
sentences.s249-s17: A hundred camels bore all his jewels; a hundred camels bore all the army's garments.
sentences.s249-s18: From much shouting and exultation and pauses came, possessing them, the trumpets of Tahmuras the virtuous.
sentences.s249-s19: He bore the palanquin from city to city; he went on until he reached the city of Turan.
sentences.s249-s20: When he came near the city of Khotan, a spectacle appeared before him — men and women came to look.
sentences.s249-s33: If a champion takes me captive under his power, (I will give) my fine steed and sell my jewel.
sentences.s249-s34: I will neither ask anyone for your justice, nor will I make jewels fall from the cloud of your favour.
sentences.s249-s35: Then he set that regal cup, full of gems, among the kings and offered it before him.
sentences.s249-s36: Precious Arab‑bred horses, upon whose manes the wind scattered dust.
sentences.s249-s1: When the commander of the turn, Badr, arrived, they girded the troop's belts for the night.
sentences.s249-s2: All the spearmen and warriors, and all the frontier-guards — the famed ones.
sentences.s249-s3: All the spears and arrows carried by the beasts — all, in the battle, had their hands washed in blood.
sentences.s249-s4: At dawn's edge, at the cock's cry, they beat the great drum on the mountain.
sentences.s249-s25: He gave (it) to the obedient, and himself went forward; he strode proudly into the presence of the elders (with a proud flourish).
sentences.s249-s26: He uttered praise, saying, “O famed Kay — (may) fortune and skill be for Iran and Turan.”
sentences.s249-s27: He so altered his face that he made him look like a sovereign, so much so that the elders did not know him again.
sentences.s249-s28: They asked and said, “From where are you? Tell us: what sort of man are you, and how did you come? — swiftly, swiftly!”
sentences.s249-s37: He praised (him) greatly, granted that request to him, and matters became arranged.
sentences.s249-s38: When the elders looked upon those jewels, from which the shining cup came forth.
sentences.s249-s39: Thereupon he praised and struck/played it, and set it upon that turquoise-adorned throne.
sentences.s249-s40: so that his face be joyful and secure in the city of Andra; now let us make a place near him.
sentences.s249-s29: Rostam said to him, “I am lesser than you; may God make your city my watering-place.”
sentences.s249-s30: As a merchant from Iran, I traversed this difficult and distant road in such a way.
sentences.s249-s31: I am a seller and also a buyer; I sell and I buy things of every kind.
sentences.s249-s32: With your kindness I hold for my soul a promise; thus hope became dominant upon my heart.
sentences.s249-s49: Buyers on every side pricked up their ears when news came from the jewel-seller.
sentences.s249-s50: The buyers laid brocade, carpets, and jewels before the elders' presence and bowed their heads.
sentences.s249-s51: When you, like the sun, arrayed the world, you rose toward that market hut.
sentences.s249-s45: Since there were people of every kind with us, that jewel must not be lost.
sentences.s249-s46: He said to him, "Turn toward your desire and take a place; I will act as your guide before you and at your feet."
sentences.s249-s47: Someone chose a house and built upon it; he put his baggage into the cottage and set down the load.
sentences.s249-s48: News came that a caravan from Iran had arrived to the renowned champion.
sentences.s249-s13: Merchants, bring all the garments; put them on and loosen the strap from the middle.
sentences.s249-s14: The troop's silver belts were wide; clothe them in kilim garments.
sentences.s249-s15: They turned their faces toward the city of Turan—one caravan, full of color and scent.
sentences.s249-s16: Seven precious horses accompanied the caravan; one was Rakhsh, and another sat likewise.
